title: SEGMENT 14 — Adenovirus vs AAV: when to use which

lines.text: SEGMENT 14 — Adenovirus vs AAV: when to use which
You can't really understand adenovirus as a vector without understanding its main competitor for in vivo gene delivery, which is AAV.

We'll spend a segment doing a direct comparison, because in practice the choice between these two vectors is one of the most consequential decisions in any in vivo gene therapy program, and the trade-offs matter.
Let me lay out the key axes of comparison.
Cargo capacity.
AAV: about 4.7 kb of single-stranded DNA, and that's the hard limit — bigger inserts simply don't package efficiently.
Adenovirus: 8 kb for first-generation, up to 36 kb for gutless.
If your gene of interest plus regulatory elements exceeds 4.5 kb, AAV is out and adenovirus or some other vector is your only option.
For a small gene like factor IX (used in hemophilia B gene therapy), AAV is fine.

For a huge gene like dystrophin — which encodes the protein deficient in Duchenne muscular dystrophy and is one of the longest human genes — AAV simply cannot fit the full-length gene, and you have to deliver truncated mini-dystrophin or use a larger vector.
Genome behavior in target cells.
AAV: predominantly episomal, like adenovirus, with a small fraction of insertional integration that has become a subject of intense study.
Adenovirus: episomal, no integration.
Both vectors are non-integrating in practice, which is a safety advantage over lentiviral and retroviral vectors that integrate by design.
Duration of expression.
AAV in non-dividing cells: expression can be sustained for years.
AAV in dividing cells: expression is gradually diluted as cells divide and lose episomal copies.
First-generation adenovirus: a few weeks at most, due to immune clearance of transduced cells.
Gutless adenovirus: months to years, comparable to AAV in many tissues.
Immune response.
AAV: low to moderate; pre-existing antibodies are a problem for some serotypes (AAV2 is common; AAV9 less so), and capsid-specific T cell responses are real but generally manageable.
Adenovirus: high, as we have discussed at length.
AAV is the quieter, lower-acuity vector.
Tropism.
Both have natural tropisms determined by their capsid surface, and both have been extensively engineered to alter those tropisms.

AAV has a much larger range of natural serotypes with diverse tropisms — AAV2 for many tissues, AAV9 for muscle and CNS, AAV8 for liver, AAVrh10 for various — and the AAV community has been particularly active in directed evolution of capsids to find novel tropisms.
Adenovirus has fewer commonly used serotypes for vector use, though the chimeric and re-targeted variants discussed earlier expand the repertoire.
Manufacturing.
AAV: traditionally challenging and expensive at scale, though manufacturing capacity has expanded significantly in the last decade.
Adenovirus: easier and cheaper at scale, with well-established producer cell lines and purification protocols.
Cost per dose.
AAV doses for in vivo gene therapy are extraordinarily expensive — multi-million-dollar treatments are common for AAV-based therapies.
Adenovirus doses for vaccines are cents to a few dollars at scale, partly because manufacturing is cheaper and partly because doses are much smaller per recipient.
So when do you pick which?
Here are some heuristics.

Pick AAV when: the cargo is small (under ~4.5 kb); you want sustained expression in a non-dividing tissue (liver, muscle, retina, CNS); the patient population doesn't have prohibitive pre-existing anti-capsid immunity; you can afford the manufacturing and dosing costs; you only need to deliver once.

Pick adenovirus when: the cargo is large (over 4.5 kb); you want transient expression of an antigen (vaccines); you want to drive a strong T cell response (vaccines, some cancer therapies); you need cheaper manufacturing at scale; you want to deliver something that actively benefits from triggering inflammation (oncolytics, certain vaccines).
Pick neither — pick lentivirus — when: you need permanent integration into the host genome (CAR-T manufacturing, some HSC gene therapies); you want stable transduction of dividing cells.
The vector choice is not a fashion judgment.
Each tool has a real envelope of use cases, and the engineering history of the last forty years has clarified those envelopes considerably.
AAV captured most of in vivo gene therapy in the 2010s; adenovirus captured most of viral-vector vaccines in the 2020s; lentivirus captured most of ex vivo cell engineering.
They coexist in the toolkit.
Which one you pick is determined by the job, not the brand.
